planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in moist to wet, well-drained to moderately wet, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in full sun to part shade; shadblow serviceberry is a native multi-stemmed deciduous shrub or small tree of wetlands, streambanks, and moist woodland edges throughout the Northeast — named for the shad fish that run upstream when it flowers in earliest spring, producing spectacular clouds of white flowers followed by sweet, blueberry-like berries in early summer.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in full sun to part shade; shadblow tolerates wet, poorly drained sites better than most Amelanchier species — excellent for rain gardens, streambanks, and moist woodland edges. Amelanchier canadensis (shadblow serviceberry or downy serviceberry) is one of the most widespread native Amelanchier species of the eastern United States, growing naturally in bogs, streambanks, wet woods, and swampy thickets from Maine to Georgia and inland to the Great Lakes. The plant flowers in earliest spring — often the first woody plant to bloom, sometimes even before forsythia — with spectacular clusters of pure white, 5-petaled flowers on bare branches that open as the leaves begin to emerge. The flowers are followed in June by small (1/3-inch), round, purple-black to dark blue-purple berries with a mild, sweet, almond-apple flavor distinctly different from blueberries — produced abundantly enough that a mature shrub can yield several quarts of fruit. The berries are excellent fresh, in pies, jams, pancake syrup, and anywhere blueberries are used. The name 'shadblow' (also written shad-bush or shad-blow) derives from the colonial observation that the tree bloomed when alewife and shad fish began running up rivers from the sea — a reliable calendar indicator for early spring fishing. Notable for better tolerance of wet soils than smooth serviceberry (A. laevis).
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Amelanchier canadensis
University of Maine Cooperative Extension — Serviceberry

planting.note: Plant container stock or bare-root trees in early spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy to clay-loam soil in full sun; cherry plum is one of the earliest-flowering and most adaptable small fruit trees, native to Western Asia but naturalized throughout Europe — it serves as one of the primary rootstocks for grafted plums worldwide, and the edible cherry-sized fruits (red, yellow, or purple) can be used like small plums for eating fresh, making jam, and flavoring wine.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; self-fertile but produces better with a second tree for cross-pollination; cherry plum is extremely adaptable and flowers early. Prunus cerasifera (cherry plum or myrobalan) is a small deciduous tree native to Western Asia and southeastern Europe — one of the earliest flowering Prunus species in spring, often opening white or pale-pink blossoms in late winter or very early spring even before peaches or almonds, creating a spectacular early-season display. The cherry-sized (3/4–1 inch) fruits ripen in summer in a range of colors from bright yellow to red to dark purple-black depending on variety, with sweet to sweet-tart, plum-like flesh that can be eaten fresh, cooked into jam, preserved as conserves, or fermented into wine or vinegar. Cherry plum is enormously important horticulturally as one of the primary rootstocks onto which commercial plum, apricot, and nectarine varieties are grafted. Several ornamental purple-leaved forms (particularly 'Atropurpurea' and the popular 'Thundercloud' and 'Purple Pony') are among the most commonly planted street and specimen trees in zone 5–9 landscapes for their deep maroon-purple spring foliage and masses of pink flowers. The species naturalizes readily and has escaped into the wild in parts of North America and Europe.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Stone Fruit Rootstocks

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in spring or fall in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, clay to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; midland hawthorn is the woodland-edge species of hawthorn native to the deciduous forests of Europe — slightly more shade-tolerant and adapted to heavier soils than common hawthorn (C. monogyna) — producing abundant dark-red to deep-scarlet haws (berries) used for jelly, wine, and hawthorn cordial.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; midland hawthorn tolerates heavier clay soils and partial shade better than common hawthorn. Crataegus laevigata (midland hawthorn or English May) is a native European hawthorn species closely related to — and often confused with — common hawthorn (C. monogyna), but botanically distinguished by having 2–3 styles per flower (instead of one in C. monogyna), deeply lobed but less deeply cut leaves, and preferring the partial-shade, moist-soil woodland-edge habitat rather than open scrub and hedgerows. Both species are called 'May' or 'May blossom' in England, referring to the masses of white (or in cultivated forms, pink or red) flowers in May that were traditionally brought into homes for May Day celebrations. The deep-red to scarlet fruits (haws) are similar to those of C. monogyna: small, oval, containing 2–3 seeds, with a mild, floury, slightly sweet-tart flavor. In European herbal tradition, hawthorn berries and flowers are among the most important cardiovascular herbs — widely used in European phytomedicine for mild heart conditions, improving coronary blood flow, and supporting healthy blood pressure. The double-flowered pink ornamental forms ('Paul's Scarlet' and 'Rosea Flore Pleno') are among the most popular ornamental flowering trees in British gardens.
sources: Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

planting.note: Plant grafted container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), deep, loamy soil in full sun; golden kiwi (A. chinensis) is a separate species from the fuzzy kiwifruit (A. deliciosa) — distinguished by its smooth golden-yellow skin, golden-yellow to bright-yellow flesh, and sweeter, less acidic flavor — the source of Zespri Gold and similar gold kiwifruit commercial varieties; requires 1 male plant per 6–8 female plants for pollination; plant 2 or more for fruit.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), deep, loamy soil in full sun; provide a very sturdy trellis or pergola — mature vines are extremely vigorous and heavy; one male plant pollinates up to 8 female plants. Actinidia chinensis (golden kiwi or yellow kiwifruit) is a separate species from the common fuzzy kiwifruit (A. deliciosa), native to the forests of central and eastern China — though the two species are closely related and sometimes hybridized commercially. The golden kiwi is distinguished from fuzzy kiwifruit by its smooth, thin, golden-yellow to bronze skin (no fuzzy brown hairs), golden-yellow to bright-orange-yellow flesh, and distinctly sweeter, tropical, less acidic flavor profile with notes of mango, papaya, and vanilla in addition to the classic kiwifruit taste. Golden kiwis are now among the most commercially valuable kiwifruit worldwide; the New Zealand-bred 'Zespri SunGold' variety (patented) dominates the premium market, but non-patented gold kiwi cultivars are available to home growers. Like fuzzy kiwifruit, A. chinensis is a vigorous deciduous vine requiring a substantial support structure, annual dormant pruning, and separate male and female plants (dioecious). It is somewhat less cold-hardy than A. deliciosa and requires mild winters without late spring frosts to protect the early-emerging shoots.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Kiwifruit Growing
California Rare Fruit Growers — Golden Kiwi
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Actinidia chinensis

planting.note: Direct sow in spring (direct seeding preferred — fennel taproot does not transplant well) in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, full-sun soil; fennel is a Mediterranean perennial herb that produces aromatic, feathery anise-flavored foliage, edible yellow flower heads, and flavorful seeds — the fronds, seeds, and (in Florence fennel var. azoricum) the bulb are all culinary staples used across European, Indian, and Middle Eastern cuisines; avoid planting near tomatoes, kohlrabi, or peppers, which are allelopathically suppressed by fennel root exudates.

directions: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, lean, full-sun soil; does not transplant well due to taproot — sow in place; in zone 7+, often self-sows prolifically. Foeniculum vulgare (fennel or sweet fennel) is one of the most versatile and widely used culinary herbs in the world — a perennial (often treated as a biennial) Mediterranean herb producing delicate, feathery, bright-green to bronze-purple fronds, hollow stems, and golden-yellow flat-topped flower umbels followed by aromatic oval seeds. The distinctive, sweet-anise, warm-licorice flavor of fennel fronds, seeds, flowers, and the swollen stem base pervades cuisines worldwide: Italian sausage, Provençal bouillabaisse, Indian chai spice, Bengali panch phoron (five-spice), Indian paan, Middle Eastern bread, German sauerkraut, and Scandinavian aquavit all rely on fennel. Florence fennel (var. azoricum) produces the fat, crisp, sweet, anise-flavored stem base eaten as a vegetable across Mediterranean Europe. The dried seeds are one of the most important spices in Italian cuisine and make a classic digestive tea. COMPANION PLANTING CAUTION: Fennel produces allelopathic compounds from its roots that stunt the growth of tomatoes, peppers, kohlrabi, and many other vegetables — plant it in a separate herb bed away from the vegetable garden or at the garden perimeter.
sources: Herb Society of America — Foeniculum vulgare
University of California Cooperative Extension — Culinary Herbs
National Center for Home Food Preservation — Fennel

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks after last frost when soil reaches 70°F in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, loamy soil in full sun; soak seeds 12–24 hours in warm water before planting to speed germination; Red Burgundy is an AAS award-winning heirloom okra variety producing deeply colored deep-red to burgundy-purple pods that are as ornamental as they are edible — the pods maintain their color fresh but turn green when cooked.

directions: Direct sow after soil warms to 70°F+ in full sun, well-drained, moderately fertile soil; harvest pods at 3–4 inches for best tenderness; Red Burgundy produces excellent fresh pods and makes a very beautiful ornamental edible garden plant. Abelmoschus esculentus 'Red Burgundy' is an All-America Selections award-winning heirloom okra variety selected for its striking deep red-burgundy to near-black pod color — one of the most visually dramatic okra varieties available. The compact, 4–5-foot plants produce deep-red stems, leaf veins, and pods that create a stunning ornamental effect in the edible garden or mixed border. The pods are tender, flavorful, and productive at 3–4 inches; they have the same culinary properties as green okra but with the added visual appeal of the deep color. The color is caused by anthocyanin pigments, the same antioxidant compounds found in red cabbage and purple sweet potatoes; like other anthocyanin-rich vegetables, Red Burgundy okra pods lose most of their red color and turn green when cooked, though the anthocyanins are still consumed. The flavor and texture are essentially identical to high-quality green okra. Red Burgundy is productive in the long, hot Gulf Coast summer and was a 1988 AAS winner — one of the most enduringly popular colored okra varieties in American seed catalogs.
sources: All-America Selections — Red Burgundy Okra (1988 Award Winner)
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Okra
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Heirloom Okra Varieties
